petitioner_background: The Petitioner is a paralegal originally from Kazakhstan, where she studied international law. Since 2021, she has worked for a New York law firm, first as an intern and later as a paralegal. Her stated ultimate intention is to establish her own law firm upon completing a master's degree program in the United States. She seeks classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under INA § 203(b)(1)(A).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted letters and certificates expressing gratitude for her charitable and volunteer work.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that these documents 'are not awards or prizes.' On appeal, the Petitioner did not address the Director's conclusions regarding claimed prizes, therefore waiving appeal on this issue. The AAO cited Matter of O-R-E-, 28 I&N Dec. 330, 336 n.5 (BIA 2021) for the principle that any ground of ineligibility not raised on appeal is waived.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted a screen capture showing her and three other individuals at a Russian-language radio station in New York.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded that, without a transcript of the video appearance, the Petitioner had not established that the video amounted to published material about her relating to her work in her field. On appeal, the Petitioner did not address the Director's conclusions regarding published material, therefore waiving appeal on this issue.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner claimed to satisfy this criterion based on membership in associations that require outstanding achievements, but specific evidence is not detailed in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director did not make any merits determinations relating to this criterion, instead concluding that the practice of law does not fall within the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics enumerated in section 203(b)(1)(A). The AAO disagreed with the Director's categorical exclusion and remanded for the Director to make a determination in the first instance regarding this criterion. The AAO took no position on whether the criterion was satisfied.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner claimed to satisfy this criterion based on original contributions of major significance, but specific evidence is not detailed in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director did not make any merits determinations relating to this criterion, instead concluding that the practice of law does not fall within the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics enumerated in section 203(b)(1)(A). The AAO disagreed with the Director's categorical exclusion and remanded for the Director to make a determination in the first instance regarding this criterion. The AAO took no position on whether the criterion was satisfied.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner claimed to satisfy this criterion based on a leading or critical role for distinguished organizations or establishments, but specific evidence is not detailed in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director did not make any merits determinations relating to this criterion, instead concluding that the practice of law does not fall within the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics enumerated in section 203(b)(1)(A). The AAO disagreed with the Director's categorical exclusion and remanded for the Director to make a determination in the first instance regarding this criterion. The AAO took no position on whether the criterion was satisfied.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner claimed to satisfy this criterion based on high remuneration for services, but specific evidence is not detailed in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director did not make any merits determinations relating to this criterion, instead concluding that the practice of law does not fall within the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics enumerated in section 203(b)(1)(A). The AAO disagreed with the Director's categorical exclusion and remanded for the Director to make a determination in the first instance regarding this criterion. The AAO took no position on whether the criterion was satisfied.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO withdrew the Director's decision and remanded the matter for entry of a new decision. The AAO disagreed with the Director's conclusion that the practice of law cannot qualify for extraordinary ability classification because it is a profession not falling within 'the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics.' Citing 20 C.F.R. § 656.5(b)(1) and Gulen v. Chertoff, the AAO held that the practice of law falls within the understanding of 'science or art,' and the broader field of law may fall under 'business.' Given this ambiguity, the AAO remanded for the Director to make first-instance merits determinations on the four remaining claimed criteria (membership, original contributions, leading role, and high remuneration). The AAO emphasized that the remand was for procedural reasons only and was not a determination of eligibility.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537, 537 n.2 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Matter of O-R-E-, 28 I&N Dec. 330, 336 n.5 (BIA 2021)
Matter of R-A-M-, 25 I&N Dec. 657, 658 n.2 (BIA 2012)
Gulen v. Chertoff, No. CIV.A.07-2148, 2008 WL 2779001 (E.D. Pa. July 16, 2008)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: The Director erroneously concluded that the practice of law, as a profession, categorically does not fall within 'the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics' enumerated in section 203(b)(1)(A), and therefore failed to make merits determinations on four of the six claimed criteria. The AAO found this was legal error and remanded for first-instance merits determinations. Additionally, the Petitioner waived appeal on two criteria (awards and published material) by failing to address the Director's conclusions on those issues.
